elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{CORDIC Arcsine}\label{al:arcsin} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Procedure{Arcsin}{$t$, $n$}\Comment{$t \in [-1,1]$} \State $ang \gets 0$, $x \gets 1$, $y \gets 0$, $t \gets t, d \gets 0$ \label{line:asin-init} \For{$i=1, i<n, ++i$} \State { $d_i \gets [s(x_i) \wedge s(t_i-y_i)] \oplus s(x_i) \oplus [s(x_i) \wedge s(y_i)] \oplus s(t_i-y_i)$\label{line:asin-dUpdate}} \If{$d_i$} $x, y \gets y, x$ \EndIf \For{\_ in range(2)} \State $x, y \gets x-2^{-i}y, y+2^{-i}x$ \EndFor \If{$d_i$} $x, y \gets y, x$ \EndIf \State $t \gets (1+2^{-2i})t$ \State $ang \gets ang + (-1)^{d_i}2\arctan{2^{-i}}$ \label{line:asin-thetaUpdate} \EndFor \State return $ang$ \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Mult($in,aux,m$): $in\gets (1+2^{-m})in$}\label{al:mult} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Procedure{Mult}{$in$, aux, $m$} \Comment{$in$ is a register of size $n$, aux is a auxiliary register of size $n$ with near 0 value, $m$ is the right shift} \State{F $\gets [1,1,2,3,5,8,13,\dots]$} \Comment{Fibbonacci Sequence} \State{$\#iter \gets \left\lceil\sqrt{5}\varphi^{-m}n\right\rceil$} \Comment{$\varphi = (1+\sqrt{5})/2$} \State{$aux \gets aux+in$} \label{line:initStart} \For{$i=\#iter$, $i\geq 0$, $--i$} \label{line:unbuildX} \If{$i$ even} \State $in \gets in-(-1)^{F[i]}(aux\gg(m\cdot F[i]))$ \Else \State $aux \gets aux-(-1)^{F[i]}(in\gg(m\cdot F[i]))$ \EndIf \EndFor \State $aux \gets aux-in$\label{line:emptyAux2} \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Div($in,aux,m$): $in \gets (1+2^{-m})^{-1}in$}\label{al:div} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Procedure{Div}{$in$, aux, $m$} \Comment{$in$ is a register of size $n$, aux is a auxiliary register of size $n$ with near 0 value, $m$ is the right shift} \State{F $\gets [1,1,2,3,5,8,13,\dots]$} \Comment{Fibbonacci Sequence} \State{$\#iter \gets \left\lceil\sqrt{5}\varphi^{-m}n\right\rceil$} \Comment{$\varphi = (1+\sqrt{5})/2$} \State{$aux \gets aux+in$} \label{line:initStart} \For{$i=0$, $i < \#iter$, $++i$} \label{line:buildX} \If{$i$ even} \label{line:ifIeven} \State $aux \gets aux+(-1)^{F[i]}(in\gg(m\cdot F[i]))$ \Else \State $in \gets in+(-1)^{F[i]}(aux\gg(m\cdot F[i]))$ \EndIf \EndFor \State $aux \gets aux-in$\label{line:div-EmptyAux} \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-001: This work introduces a quantum algorithm for computing the arcsine function to an arbitrary accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-002: We leverage a technique from embedded computing and field-programmable gate array (FPGA), called COordinate Rotation DIgital Computer (CORDIC).
elements.sent-2411-003: CORDIC is a family of iterative algorithms that, in a classical context, can approximate various trigonometric, hyperbolic, and elementary functions using only bit shifts and additions.
elements.sent-2411-004: Adapting CORDIC to the quantum context is non-trivial, as the algorithm traditionally uses several non-reversible operations.
elements.sent-2411-005: We detail a method for CORDIC which avoids such non-reversible operations.
elements.sent-2411-006: We propose multiple approaches to calculate the arcsine function reversibly with CORDIC.
elements.sent-2411-007: For n bits of precision, our method has space complexity of order n qubits, a layer count in the order of n times log n, and a CNOT count in the order of n squared.
elements.sent-2411-008: This primitive function is a required step for the Harrow–Hassidim–Lloyd (HHL) algorithm, is necessary for quantum digital-to-analog conversion, can simplify a quantum speed-up for Monte-Carlo methods, and has direct applications in the quantum estimation of Shapley values.
elements.sent-2411-009: The problem of quantum digital-to-amplitude conversion \cite{mitarai2019quantum is an essential step in various quantum algorithms, including \gls{hhl --- a quantum algorithm for solving linear equations~\cite{harrow2009quantum , a quantum speed-up for Monte Carlo methods \cite{montanaro2015quantum , and a quantum algorithm for Shapley value estimation \cite{burge2023quantum .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, to efficiently perform this conversion it is necessary to calculate inverse trigonometric functions, which is computationally expensive \cite{mitarai2019quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-011: As quantum computation is very young, it is valuable to consider techniques used in early classical computing intended for weak hardware.
elements.sent-2411-012: In particular, this work adapts the classical CORDIC algorithm for arcsin to the quantum setting.
elements.sent-2411-014: Sections~\ref{sec:preliminaries and~\ref{sec:cCORDIC provide preliminaries to our problem, and introduces the approach of classical CORDIC to approximate arcsin on minimal hardware.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Section~\ref{sec:qCORDIC translates the approach to a quantum setting.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Section~\ref{sec:tranImplem demonstrates the use of quantum CORDIC to perform a quantum digital-to-analogue transformation.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Section~\ref{sec:complexity gives a sketch proof of the time and space complexity of quantum CORDIC and provides simulation results.}
elements.sent-2411-019: There have been a few attempts to design a quantum algorithm for the arcsin function.
elements.sent-2411-020: For instance, H\"{a ner et al.~\cite{haner2018optimizing leverages quantum parallelism to perform piecewise polynomial approximations of a wide range of functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: While the approach is flexible, it is not ideal in the short term, since it requires some memory access, a fair number of multiplications, and square rooting for the extreme portions of $\arcsin{x}$, i.e., $x\in [-1,-0.5]\cup[0.5,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-022: It can be seen as a promising solution that scales well in precise situations, e.g., more than $32$ bits.
elements.sent-2411-023: There also exists an iterative method~\cite{wang2020quantum , which performs a binary search to approximate $\arcsin{x}$, among other elementary functions.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The suggested solution is very general.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, it requires many squaring operations that are challenging to implement in the near term.
elements.sent-2411-026: The above techniques are valuable for various applications when sufficient hardware is available.
elements.sent-2411-027: In the late 1950s, Volder~\cite{volder1959cordic introduced the CORDIC algorithm to solve an important family of trigonometric equations using the underpowered hardware of the time.}
elements.sent-2411-028: This was later generalized to a single algorithm that solves various elementary functions, including multiplication, division, the trigonometric functions, arctan, the hyperbolic trigonometric functions, $\ln$, $\exp$, and square root \cite{walther1971unified .}
elements.sent-2411-029: CORDIC has been further modified to solve various other functions, including our target function, arcsin~\cite{mazenc1993computing .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Suppose you have the following $(n+1)$-qubit quantum state: \begin{equation}\ket{\phi} = \sum\limits_{k=0}^{L-1} \alpha_k \ket{h_k}_in \ket{0}_out,\end{equation} where $L\leq 2^{n}$, the in register has size $n$, the out register is of size one, and $h_k$ represents a number in range $[0,1]$ as an $n$-bit binary string.
elements.sent-2411-031: A particularly useful transformation on this state is given by the following equation: \begin{equation}\label{eq:goalTransform} U\ket{\phi} = \sum\limits_{k=0}^{L-1} \alpha_k \ket{h_k}_in \left(\sqrt{1-h_k} \ket{0} + \sqrt{h_k} \ket{1}\right)_out.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-032: This transformation is an important instance of quantum digital-to-analog conversion~\cite{mitarai2019quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-033: A fast solution to this problem is valuable in multiple algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-034: For instance, a quantum algorithm for approximating Shapley values \cite{burge2023quantum could leverage this transformation to simplify the state preparation step.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Another important example is the quantum speedup of Monte Carlo methods \cite{montanaro2015quantum which requires the transformation $W$, defined as: \begin{equation}\ket{x}_in\ket{0}_out \xrightarrow{W} \ket{x}_in\left(\sqrt{1-\Phi(x)}\ket{0} + \sqrt{\Phi(x)}\ket{1} \right)_out.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-036: This can be translated easily to our transformation.
elements.sent-2411-037: We split the computation into two steps, where we first implement a reversible algorithm to compute $\Phi$ in the computational basis, and then apply our transformation.
elements.sent-2411-038: Take register in to have $m$ qubits, the register aux to have $n$ qubits, and the register out to have one qubit.
elements.sent-2411-039: We first apply the unitary $V$ using the in register as input and outputting the $n$-bit approximate result $\tilde{\Phi}$ to aux, as follows: \begin{equation}\ket{x}_in\ket{0}_aux \xrightarrow{V} \ket{x}_in\ket{\Tilde{\Phi}(x)}_aux.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-040: Then, $W$ can be constructed with $n$ auxiliary qubits by composing $V$, and $U$, Equation~\eqref{eq:goalTransform , where the aux acts as the input.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Finally, to restore the aux register to its initial state, we apply the $V^{-1}$ transformation.
elements.sent-2411-042: In summary, we can solve the problem using a reversible classical algorithm implementation of $\tilde{\Phi}$ followed by encoding the result into the probability amplitude of the output bit.
elements.sent-2411-043: Another practical use case is as a step of the \gls*{hhl algorithm~\cite{harrow2009quantum .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: In this case, reciprocal eigenvalues are encoded as binary strings in a superposition.
elements.sent-2411-045: An essential step is to encode the reciprocals of the eigenvalues in the probability amplitudes of an output bit.
elements.sent-2411-046: In particular, we can apply a transformation closely related to $U$ from Equation~\eqref{eq:goalTransform .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Hence, an efficient algorithm to implement the transformation $U$, Equation~\eqref{eq:goalTransform , has immediate benefits to foundational problems.}
elements.sent-2411-048: There are a few naive approaches to implementing the transformation.
elements.sent-2411-049: One is to use a lookup table, where each possible input $\ket{h_k _in$ is separately implemented, but that would require exponential circuit depth.}
elements.sent-2411-050: With some naive methods out of the way, we examine a less trivial direction that illustrates the main challenge solved by this paper.
elements.sent-2411-052: We require $h_k$ to be in range $[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Suppose we apply the function $\arcsin$ where in is the input register, and aux is the output register.
elements.sent-2411-054: Applying this unitary, which we call $F$, yields, \begin{equation}F\ket{\psi_0} = \ket{\psi_1} = \sum\limits^{L-1}_{k=0} \alpha_k \ket{h_k}_in \ket{\arcsin\sqrt{h_k}}_aux \ket{0}_out.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-055: Suppose now that we apply the quantum circuit $R$ from Figure~\ref{fig:encodeBinaryAsAmplitude .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Using trigonometry identities, we have that, \begin{scriptsize \begin{equation}\ket{\psi_2} = \sum\limits^{L-1}_{k=0} \alpha_k \ket{h_k}_in \ket{\arcsin\sqrt{h_k}}_aux \cdot \left(\sqrt{1-h_k} \ket{0} + \sqrt{h_k} \ket{1}\right)_out.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-058: This approach transforms our previous problem into a problem of seemingly similar difficulty, computing $\arcsin$ reversibly.
elements.sent-2411-059: However, this paper shows a method to achieve this task efficiently.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \begin{adjustbox}{width=\columnwidth} \begin{tikzpicture} \node[scale=1] { \begin{quantikz} \lstick[wires=4]{$\ket{\arcsin\sqrt{h_k}}_aux$} \ctrl{4} \qw \qw \qw \qw \qw \qw \qw \ctrl{3} \qw \qw \qw \qw \qw \setwiretype{n}\vdots \ddots \qw \qw \qw \qw \qw \ctrl{1} \qw \lstick{$\ket{0}_out$} \gate{R_{y}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)} \gate{R_{y}\left(\frac{\pi}{8}\right)} \qw \setwiretype{n}\cdots \setwiretype{q} \gate{R_{y}\left(\frac{\pi}{2^{n+1}}\right)} \end{quantikz} }; \end{tikzpicture} \end{adjustbox} \end{center} \caption{Unitary transformation $R$ which encodes the binary value of the aux register into the output register. Define \hbox{$R_{y}(\omega)=(\cos\omega, -\sin\omega; \sin\omega, \cos\omega)$}. } \label{fig:encodeBinaryAsAmplitude}
elements.sent-2411-060: Let us give a brief insight into how CORDIC works.
elements.sent-2411-061: First, there is an implicit two-dimensional goal vector that encodes the problem.
elements.sent-2411-062: The exact vector is unknown, but we have a method to compare it to other vectors.
elements.sent-2411-063: A starting vector rotates towards the goal in increasingly small discrete steps, picking the direction of rotation based on our comparison method, until it converges with satisfactory error.
elements.sent-2411-064: It is possible to avoid performing any multiplications during the rotations by using pseudo-rotations, rotations with a small stretch.
elements.sent-2411-065: Our proposed method is based on Mazenc et al.'s algorithm~\cite{mazenc1993computing (but Step~1 is slightly modified, as there was an error in the original paper).}
elements.sent-2411-066: The procedure is fully described in Algorithm~1.
elements.sent-2411-067: Each iteration follows the steps below:.
elements.sent-2411-068: We first initialize the problem, given input $t\in[-1,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-069: Our goal is to get the vector $(x_i,y_i)$ to point in the same direction as the vector of length $1$ with height $t$, i.e., vector $(\sqrt{1-t^2 ,t)$.}
elements.sent-2411-070: This goal vector has an angle of $\theta=\arcsin(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-071: By keeping track of each rotation we make, assuming $(x_i,y_i)$ points in roughly the same direction as the goal vector, we can construct an approximation for $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-072: In particular, assuming infinite precision, as $i$ tends to infinity, approximation $\theta_i$ tends to $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-073: According to Ref.~\cite{mazenc1993computing , to achieve $n$ bits of accuracy it takes $n+\mathcal{O (1)$ iterations.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Step~1 is a computationally convenient way to check if $\theta_i < \theta$, which we store in variable $d_i$.
elements.sent-2411-075: If $d_i=0$, then we need to rotate counterclockwise to get closer to $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Otherwise, if $d_i=1$, we need to rotate clockwise.
elements.sent-2411-077: Steps~2 and~4 conjugate Step~3 to achieve the correct rotation direction.
elements.sent-2411-078: Step~3 performs a pseudo-rotation, slightly stretching our vector $(x_i, y_i)$ by a factor of $1+2^{-2i}$.
elements.sent-2411-079: It is performed simply by making the following substitutions twice: $x'=x-2^{-i y$ and $y'=y+2^{-i x$.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: As a result of the operation, we point closer to the goal direction, but it is necessary to compensate for the stretch.
elements.sent-2411-081: Step~5 deals with the stretching of the vector $(x_i,y_i)$ by stretching our goal vector by the same amount, $(1+2^{-2i )$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Note that multiplication by powers of $2$ can be done efficiently through bit-shifting.
elements.sent-2411-083: Finally, Step~6 records the change to the current angle of vector $(x_i,y_i)$.
elements.sent-2411-084: Note that the expression $2\arctan2^{-i}$ can be precomputed and directly encoded into hardware.
elements.sent-2411-085: The big takeaway is that each iteration uses only about five bitshifts and seven additions, regardless of the number of bits.
elements.sent-2411-086: There are a few immediate challenges that appear when we try and move CORDIC to a quantum setting, in this section we detail the solutions step-by-step.
elements.sent-2411-087: Before going through the steps, we have a few important facts to note.
elements.sent-2411-088: We represent the values $\theta_i, x_i, y_i, t_i$ using fixed point two's complement in the range $[-2,2)$ with one sign bit, one integer bit, and \hbox{$n-2$} fractional bits.
elements.sent-2411-089: This avoids any issues with overflow, in the case of $\theta_i$, $\theta$ is in the range $[-\pi/2, \pi/2)$, so $|\theta| \leq \pi/2 < 2$.
elements.sent-2411-090: In the case of $|x_i|, |y_i|, |t_i|$, each represents side-lengths of vectors with a magnitude that increases in size by $(1+4^{-i )$ each step, thus, \begin{equation}|x_i|, |y_i|, |t_i| \leq \prod\limits_{i=1}^{n} \left(1+4^{-i} \right) < \sqrt[3]{e} < 2.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-091: You can verify the inequality by taking $n\rightarrow \infty$, taking the $\ln$ of the infinite product, using the inequality \hbox{$\ln(1+x)<x$} for positive $x$, and finally using the geometric series.
elements.sent-2411-092: The product represents the stretch caused by the pseudo-rotations over the iterations.
elements.sent-2411-093: The two's complement also allows us to check if a value is negative easily using the most significant bit, if it is $1$ then the value is negative, otherwise, the value is positive.
elements.sent-2411-094: To approximate multiplication by $2^{-m}$, we will use the right bit-shift operation, $x_0x_1\cdots x_{n-1}\gg m$, we move each bit to the right by $m$ places, deleting the $m$ rightmost bits.
elements.sent-2411-095: Also note that, when right bit-shifting in two's complement, the previous leftmost bit is copied into the new leftmost bits, for example: \begin{equation}x_0 x_1 x_2 x_3 x_4 \gg 2 = x_0 x_0 x_0 x_1 x_2 , \quad x_k\in\{0,1\}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}x_0 x_1 x_2 x_3 x_4 \gg 2 = x_0 x_0 x_0 x_1 x_2 , \quad x_k\in\{0,1\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-096: This ensures right bitshifts work as a division by powers of two for negative numbers.
elements.sent-2411-097: Now, let us go through the steps of the quantum implementation, assuming $n$ bits each to represent $\theta_i, x_i, y_i, t_i$ as fixed point numbers in registers $ang, x, y, t$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-098: Allocate $n-1$ bits to represent \hbox{$d=d_1d_2\cdots d_{n-1}$} as a bit array in register d where $d_i$ represents the $i$th register qubit.
elements.sent-2411-099: Initialization is trivial, we use $t\gets t$ as the input.
elements.sent-2411-100: Note that we do not need to use new registers for each iteration, one register per value is sufficient.
elements.sent-2411-101: For Step~1, we can perform this step with the following sequence of operations, where the leftmost bit is most significant.
elements.sent-2411-102: We begin with the state: \begin{equation}\ket{0}_{d_i} \ket{x_i}_x \ket{y_i}_y \ket{t_i}_t\end{equation} We perform a subtraction (implemented with an inverse addition gate) from y to t, yielding, \begin{equation}\ket{0}_{d_i} \ket{x_i}_x \ket{y_i}_y \ket{t_i-y_i}_t.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-103: Looking directly at the sign bits of the x, y, and t registers gives $s(x_i)$, $s(y_i)$, and $s(t_i-y_i)$ respectively, where \hbox{$s(z)=1$} if $z<0$ else $s(z)=0$.
elements.sent-2411-104: We next perform the following operations,.
elements.sent-2411-105: Perform a Toffoli gate using the sign bits of the x and t registers as controls and qubit $d_i$ as the target.
elements.sent-2411-106: Perform a Toffoli gate using the sign bits of the x and y registers as controls and qubit $d_i$ as the target.
elements.sent-2411-107: Perform a CNOT gate using the sign bits of the x register as control and qubit $d_i$ as the target.
elements.sent-2411-108: Perform a CNOT gate using the sign bits of the t register as control and qubit $d_i$ as the target.
elements.sent-2411-109: Finally, we can undo the previous subtraction by adding the y register back to the t register.
elements.sent-2411-110: This gives us a correct $d_i$ bit without side effects.
elements.sent-2411-111: Steps~2 and~4 are trivial to implement using swap gates between each bit of the x and y register controlled by $d_i$, this can be done using $18n$ CNOTs.
elements.sent-2411-113: It leverages Algorithm~2 for multiplying by $(1+2^{-k )$.}
elements.sent-2411-114: Note, that each step of Algorithm~2 is reversible.
elements.sent-2411-115: An explanation for Mult is the subject of Appendix~\ref{appendix:mult .}
elements.sent-2411-116: For notational simplicity, we assume x and y have not been swapped Our initial state for this step is, \begin{equation}\ket{x_i}_x\ket{y_i}_y.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-117: We first subtract $y_i \gg i$ ($y_i$ right shifted by $i$ bits) from register x, giving us, \begin{equation}\ket{x_i-2^{-i}y_i}_x\ket{y_i}_y.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-118: Next, we need to take a mostly clean (value near 0) auxiliary register.
elements.sent-2411-119: We apply $Mult(y, aux, 2i)$ to the y register, \begin{equation}\ket{x_i-2^{-i}y_i}_x\ket{y_i+2^{-2i}y_i}_y.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-120: Finally, we add the x register shifted, $(x_i-2^{-i y_i) \gg i$, to the y register, \begin{equation}\ket{x_i-2^{-i}y_i}_x\ket{y_i+2^{-2i}y_i+2^{-i}(x_i-2^{-i}y_i)}_y.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-122: By repeating these operations once more, Step~4 is accomplished.
elements.sent-2411-123: With our new $Mult$ tool, Step~5 is trivial.
elements.sent-2411-124: We simply apply $Mult(t, aux, 2i)$ to the t register.
elements.sent-2411-125: Finally, for Step~$6$ we apply controlled negation of the $ang$ register using $d_i$ as the control.
elements.sent-2411-126: Then add the precomputed $2\arctan2^{-i}$ to the $\theta$ register, note, this operation can be encoded directly on quantum hardware.
elements.sent-2411-127: Then, once again, perform a controlled negation of the $ang$ register using $d_i$ as the control.. This gives, \begin{equation}\ket{\theta_i}_ang \rightarrow \ket{(-1)^{d_i}\theta_i}_ang \rightarrow \ket{(-1)^{d_i}\theta_i + 2\arctan2^{-i}}_ang \rightarrow \ket{(-1)^{2d_i}\theta_i + (-1)^{d_i}2\arctan2^{-i}}_ang = \ket{\theta_i + (-1)^{d_i}2\arctan2^{-i}}_ang = \ket{\theta_{i+1}}_ang.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-128: This step can be improved slightly by using bitwise NOT on the $ang$ register instead of negation.
elements.sent-2411-129: Thus, we have a quantum-compatible method for applying each step of the classical CORDIC algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-130: As we see in Section~\ref{sec:complexity , these adapted techniques add minimal error in simulation.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Suppose you are given a $5n$-qubit quantum state of the form, \begin{equation}\ket{\kappa_0} = \sum\limits_{k=0}^{L-1} \alpha_k \ket{h_k}_t \ket{0}^{\otimes n-1}_d\ket{0}^{\otimes n}_x\ket{0}^{\otimes n}_y \ket{0}^{\otimes n}_mult \ket{0}_out,\end{equation} where $L\leq N=2^{n}$, the t register has size $n$, and \hbox{$h_k\in \{0,1 \}^n$} is an $n$-bit binary string representing a value from the set \hbox{$\{2^{-n+1 j: j\in \mathbb{Z _{2^{n-1 }\}$}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: In other words, register t stores a fixed point binary number between $-2$ and $2$ in two's complement, but we restrict the initial value to be between $0$ and $1$.
elements.sent-2411-133: The registers x, y, mult are of size $n$, register d is of size $n-1$, and each is initialized to $\ket{0}$.
elements.sent-2411-134: Finally, the out register is one qubit, and will store a value corresponding to $h_k$ in its probability amplitudes.
elements.sent-2411-135: We would like to find each $d_i$, we can do this by performing the quantum implementation of the CORDIC Arcsine, with a couple modifications.
elements.sent-2411-136: To leverage Remark~\ref{remark:asinOFsqrt , we first multiply register t by two and subtract one.}
elements.sent-2411-137: Next, we set the x register equal to $1$.
elements.sent-2411-138: This gives us state, \begin{equation}\ket{\kappa_1} = \sum\limits_{k=0}^{L-1} \alpha_k \ket{h_k'}_t \ket{0}^{\otimes n}_d\ket{1}_x\ket{0}^{\otimes n}_y \ket{0}^{\otimes n}_mult \ket{0}_out,\end{equation} where $h_k'=2h_k-1 \in [-1, 1)$.
elements.sent-2411-139: We then apply Algorithm~1, skipping Line~2, since the registers are already initialized, and skipping Line~10, as we do not require an explicit representation of theta.
elements.sent-2411-141: The $j$th bit of $d^{(h'_k)}$ represents the rotation direction at iteration $j$ of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-142: Performing the inverse of Algorithm~1, where Lines~2, 4, and~10 are skipped, gives the state, \begin{equation}\ket{\kappa_3} = \sum\limits_{k=0}^{L-1} \alpha_k \ket{h_k'}_t \ket{d^{(h_k')}}_d\ket{0}_x^{\otimes n} \ket{0}_y^{\otimes n} \ket{0}_mult^{\otimes n} \ket{0}_out.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-143: By Mazenc \cite{mazenc1993computing , we have the following relation, \begin{equation}\frac{\arcsin\left(h_k'\right)}{2} \approx \sum\limits_{j=0}^{n} (-1)^{d^{\left(h_k'\right)}_j} \arctan \left(2^{-j}\right),\end{equation} with error of order $\mathcal{O (2^{-n )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: Thus, applying the transformation, \begin{scriptsize \begin{equation}R'\ket{d^{\left(h_k'\right)}}_d\ket{0}_out = \ket{d^{\left(h_k'\right)}}_d \Bigg[ \cos\left( \frac{\pi}{4} + \sum_{j=0}^{n} (-1)^{d^{\left(h_k'\right)}_j} \arctan \left(2^{-j}\right) \right) \ket{0} + \sin\left( \frac{\pi}{4} + \sum_{j=0}^{n} (-1)^{d^{\left(h_k'\right)}_j} \arctan \left(2^{-j}\right) \right)\ket{1} \Bigg]_out,\end{equation} \end{scriptsize implemented in Figure~\ref{fig:encodeDAsAmplitude , by Remark~\ref{rem:discreteBasi , gives the state, \begin{equation}\ket{\kappa_4} \approx \sum\limits_{k=0}^{L-1} \alpha_k \ket{h_k'}_t \ket{d^{(h_k')}}_d\ket{0}_x^{\otimes n} \ket{0}_y ^{\otimes n} \ket{0}_mult^{\otimes n} \otimes \Bigg[ \cos\left( \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\arcsin\left(2h_k-1\right)}{2} \right) \ket{0} + \sin\left( \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\arcsin\left(2h_k-1\right)}{2} \right)\ket{1} \Bigg]_out.\end{equation}.}}}}

elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{adjustbox}{width=\textwidth} \begin{tikzpicture} \node[scale=1] { \begin{quantikz} \lstick{$\ket{d^{(h_k')_0}}_d,0$} \ctrl{4} \qw \qw \qw \qw \lstick{$\ket{d^{(h_k')_1}}_d,1$} \ctrl{3} \qw \qw \qw \qw \setwiretype{n} \vdots \ddots \lstick{$\ket{d^{(h_k')_{n-1}}}_d,n-1$} \ctrl{1} \qw \lstick{$\ket{0}_out$} \gate{R_y\left(-2\mu_0\right)} \gate{R_y\left(\mu_0\right)} \gate{R_y\left(-2\mu_1\right)} \gate{R_y\left(\mu_1\right)} \setwiretype{n}\cdots \setwiretype{q} \gate{R_y\left(-2\mu_{n-1}\right)} \gate{R_y\left(\mu_{n-1}\right)} \gate{R_y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)} \end{quantikz} }; \end{tikzpicture} \end{adjustbox} \caption{Unitary transformation $R'$ which transfers the binary encoding of CORDIC rotation direction stored d register into the amplitude of the out register. Define \hbox{$R_{y}(\omega)=(\cos\omega, -\sin\omega; \sin\omega, \cos\omega)$}, and \hbox{$\mu_i=\tan^{-1}2^{-i}$}. } \label{fig:encodeDAsAmplitude}
elements.sent-2411-148: In this section, we detail the complexity to construct $\ket{\kappa_2}$, Equation~\eqref{eq:kappa2 .}
elements.sent-2411-149: The quantum implementation of Algorithm~1, skipping Line~10, applies Mult three times per iteration, and addition six times per iteration.
elements.sent-2411-150: Mult takes less than $\sqrt{5}\varphi^{-2i n + 2$ additions on the $i$th step if $i<n$, where $\varphi$ is the golden ratio, if $i\geq n$, then Mult can be skipped.}
elements.sent-2411-151: Thus, assuming $n$ iterations for $\mathcal{O (n)$ bits of accuracy, we perform less than, \begin{equation}3\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n/2} \left(\frac{\sqrt{5}n}{\varphi^{2i}} + 2\right) + \sum\limits_{i=1}^{n}6 = 9 n + 3\sqrt{5}n \sum\limits_{i=1}^{n/2} \frac{1}{\varphi^{2i}} < 14n,\end{equation} additions.}
elements.sent-2411-152: With $\mathcal{O (n)$ auxiliary qubits, it is possible to compute addition in $\mathcal{O (\log n)$ layers and $\mathcal{O (n)$ CNOTs \cite{takahashi2009quantum .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-153: Thus, the total layer complexity is $\mathcal{O (n\log(n))$ and the total CNOT complexity is $\mathcal{O (n^2)$ where $n$ corresponds to bits of precision.}}
elements.sent-2411-154: Note that, if $n$ is not large, it is better to use a non-parallel version of addition to minimize overhead.
elements.sent-2411-155: As for space complexity, we need $\mathcal{O (n)$ bits of space.}
elements.sent-2411-156: We require an auxiliary register of size $n$ for Mult, and optionally another register to speed up addition.
elements.sent-2411-157: We need registers of $n$ bits for x, y and t, and optionally ang.
elements.sent-2411-158: Finall, we need $n-1$ bits for register d.
elements.sent-2411-159: Thus, in total, we require a minimum of $5n-1$ qubits.
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{subfigure}{0.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.9\textwidth]{Images/a} \caption{$n=6$} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.9\textwidth]{Images/b} \caption{$n=10$} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.9\textwidth]{Images/c} \caption{$n=16$} \end{subfigure} \caption{Results of Classical simulations of Quantum compatible Algorithm for CORDIC Arcsine (see our GitHub repository at \href{https://github.com/iain-burge/QuantumCORDIC}{https://github.com/iain-burge/QuantumCORDIC} for further details).} \label{fig:Results}
elements.sent-2411-160: In simulations, it was found that the max error for any given input can be made arbitrarily small by making $n$ larger (Figure~\ref{fig:Results ).}
elements.sent-2411-161: The full codebase can be found on our GitHub companion repository~\cite{Burge_2024 , containing a complete implementation using Qiskit as well as an identical classical implementation.}
elements.sent-2411-162: We have introduced a Quantum algorithm for calculating arcsin with low space and time complexity.
elements.sent-2411-163: It also has applications in HHL (Harrow–Hassidim–Lloyd), approximating Shapley values, and quantum Monte Carlo methods.
elements.sent-2411-164: As CORDIC is most appropriate for lower precision applications, future work could apply more budget conscious implementations of CORDIC arcsin at the expense of asymptotic behavior.
elements.sent-2411-165: Further work could also develop a full CORDIC module to cheaply perform a verity of elementary functions critical for quantum computation beyond quantum digital-to-analog conversion.. .
elements.sent-2411-166: Function To understand the Mult algorithm, it is most simple to considered its inverse, Div (Algorithm~3).
elements.sent-2411-167: To simplify the following proofs, we assume infinite bits of precision in our number representations.
elements.sent-2411-168: Suppose $i$ is even, at the beginning of the $i$th iteration of Algorithm~3's \text{for loop, the state is, \begin{equation}in = z\sum_{k=0}^{F[i+1]-1} r^k,\quad aux = z\sum_{k=0}^{F[i]-1} r^k.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Base case: for $i=0$, at the beginning of the loop $in=aux=zr^0$.
elements.sent-2411-172: Inductive step: suppose that for an even \hbox{$i< \left\lceil \sqrt{5}\varphi^{-m n\right\rceil$}, the registers hold the values, \begin{equation}in = z\sum_{k=0}^{F[i+1]-1} r^k,\quad aux = z\sum_{k=0}^{F[i]-1} r^k.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-173: Hence, aux is updated to, \begin{equation}aux \gets z\sum_{k=0}^{F[i]-1} r^k + (-1)^{F[i]} \cdot 2^{m F[i]} z\sum_{k=0}^{F[i+1]-1} r^k.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-174: By definition~$(-1)^{\text{F[i] }\cdot 2^{m F[i] = r^{\text{F [i]}$, thus, the new value for aux is \hbox{$z\sum_{k=0 ^{F[i]-1 r^k + z\sum_{k=F[i] ^{F[i+1]+F[i]-1 r^k$}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-175: By definition of the Fibonacci sequence, \hbox{$F[i+2]=F[i+1]+F[i]$}, combining the summations yields, \begin{equation}aux = z\sum_{k=0}^{F[i+2]-1} r^k.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-176: On the next loop, the index register holding $i$ incremented by $1$ and is now odd, so the if statement on Line~6 gets false.
elements.sent-2411-177: Hence, the next operation updates in, \begin{equation}in \gets z\sum_{k=0}^{F[i+1]-1} r^k + (-1)^{F[i+1]} \cdot 2^{m F[i+1]} z\sum_{k=0}^{F[i+2]-1} r^k\end{equation} Similarly to the previous iteration, we find in is equal to \hbox{$z\sum_{k=0 ^{F[i+1]-1 r^k + z\sum_{k=F[i+1] ^{F[i+2]+F[i+1]-1 r^k$}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-178: Bringing the summations together shows, \begin{equation}in = z\sum_{k=0}^{F[i+3]-1} r^k .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-179: The index register is incremented once again, such that it holds $i+2$.
elements.sent-2411-181: Recall, for $r\in(0,1)$, and an integer $J$, the geometric sum formula gives, \begin{equation}\sum\limits_{k=0}^{J-1} r^k = \frac{1-r^J}{1-r} = \frac{1-2^{-Jm}}{1+2^{-m}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-182: Assuming $J$ iterations, the final state will be, \begin{equation}in \approx\frac{z}{1-2^{-m}},\quad aux \approx 0,\end{equation} with an accuracy of $\mathcal{O}\left(m\varphi^J\right)$ bits.
elements.sent-2411-183: First note the state prior to Line~10 is, \begin{equation}in = z\sum_{k=0}^{F[J+1]-1} r^k,\quad aux = z\sum_{k=0}^{F[J]-1} r^k,\end{equation} where, for simplicity, $J$ is even, greater than two, and represents the number of iterations.
elements.sent-2411-184: Then, Line~10 will result in state, \begin{equation}aux \gets z\sum_{k=0}^{F[J]-1} r^k - z\sum_{k=0}^{F[J+1]-1} r^k.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-185: This further yields aux is equal to \hbox{$- z\sum_{k=F[J] ^{F[J+1]-F[J]-1 r^k$}.}}
elements.sent-2411-186: This can be simplified to, \hbox{$-z r^{F[J] \sum_{k=0 ^{F[J-1]-1 r^k$}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: Then, the geometric sum formula gives, \begin{equation}aux = -z r^{F[J]} \frac{1-r^{F[J-1]}}{1-r}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-188: Note that $\varphi^{k-1 < F[k]$ for positive $k$.. Thus, \begin{equation}aux < -z 2^{-\varphi^{J-1}m} \frac{1-2^{-\varphi^{J-2} m }}{1-2^{-m}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-189: Note that $z$ is always less than $2$ as argued at the beginning of Section~\ref{sec:qCORDIC , and since $m\geq 1$, $J\geq 2$, we have, \hbox{$|aux| \leq 2^{-\varphi^{J-1 m + 2}$}.}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Thus, the error introduced to aux in terms of bit precision is exponentially small.
elements.sent-2411-191: On the other hand, the in register state is, \begin{equation}in = z \frac{1-2^{-mF[J]}}{1+2^{-m}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-192: Thus, we have an absolute error of less than, \hbox{$2\cdot 2^{-mF[J] \leq 2^{-m\varphi^{J-1 }$}.}}
elements.sent-2411-193: Therefore, the absolute error is exponentially small with respect to bits of accuracy.
